data.title: Genesco outlines $1.90–$2.30 EPS target for fiscal 2027 as it intensifies Journeys growth and emphasizes profitability reset at Schuh
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Genesco Inc. (GCO) Q4 2026

Management View

  • Mimi Vaughn, Chairman, President & CEO, announced the planned departure of CFO Sandra Harris and confirmed an active search for her successor, with Vaughn stepping in as Interim CFO during the transition. Vaughn stated, "We delivered a strong finish to fiscal '26 with fourth quarter results that exceeded our expectations and reflected outstanding execution during the most important shopping period of the year."
  • Vaughn highlighted a 9% increase in total comparable sales for the quarter, noting, "Stores were especially strong propelled by exceptional conversion over holiday and higher transaction size, while digital reaccelerated, especially during peak weeks." Journeys was singled out for double-digit comp gains, with its transformation credited for sustained growth and market share gains, particularly among the Team Girl demographic. She reported, "Journeys performance far outpaced the overall footwear market as the Journeys transformation gains important traction with the larger used customer base we're targeting."
  • Schuh faced a highly promotional UK retail environment, which impacted gross margins despite positive comps. Vaughn indicated a focus on restoring margin discipline and improving store productivity at Schuh in fiscal '27. Johnston & Murphy showed comp improvements, and a new partnership with Peyton Manning was highlighted as generating strong engagement.
  • Vaughn outlined a shift in strategic focus for the upcoming year, emphasizing a "Footwear First" strategy centered on four growth drivers: product, retail brand elevation, consumer experience, and team development. She also announced plans to double the number of Journeys 4.0 stores, targeting to convert about 20% of the fleet by year-end and to expand the 4.0 concept to Journeys Kids.
  • Cassandra Harris, Consultant & Principal Accounting Officer, stated, "Overall, for the quarter, we grew revenue, delivered high single-digit comps, meaningfully leveraged SG&A and generated adjusted EPS of $3.74, up $0.48 versus last year. For the full year, adjusted EPS was $1.45, finishing above our revised estimates and well ahead of the prior year."

Outlook

  • The company projects comparable sales to increase approximately 1% to 2% for fiscal '27. Vaughn stated, "We expect continued strength at Journeys, improvement at Johnston & Murphy and a reset for Schuh to drive profitability."
  • Total sales are expected to range from down 1% to flat for the year, with Schuh sales anticipated to decline mid-single digits due to planned store closures and a promotional reset, while Johnston & Murphy is projected for mid-single digit sales growth.
  • The company expects fiscal year adjusted operating income to be in the range of $32 million to $38 million and adjusted EPS to be in the range of $1.90 to $2.30.
  • Gross margin is forecast to improve by approximately 50 to 60 basis points, primarily from margin recovery at Schuh and Genesco Brands.
  • First quarter adjusted operating loss is expected to be over $1 million worse than last year, with adjusted EPS "quite a bit lower" due to tax rate impact.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter revenue was $800 million, a 7% year-over-year increase. Comparable sales rose 9%, with stores up 9% and direct up 8%.
  • Journeys' comps increased by 12%, Johnston & Murphy by 2%, and Schuh by 3%. E-commerce penetration at Schuh exceeded 50% of sales.
  • Adjusted gross margin for the quarter declined 90 basis points, with Schuh's heightened promotions as a primary factor. SG&A expense was 39.1% of sales, leveraging 140 basis points year-over-year due to cost actions and store optimization.
  • Adjusted operating income was $56 million for the quarter, up 17% from last year. Free cash flow was $164 million for Q4 and nearly $84 million for the full year.
  • The company ended the year with 42 net fewer stores, opened 4 new Johnston & Murphy stores, and had 84 Journeys 4.0 stores at year-end.

Q&A

  • Mitchel Kummetz, Seaport Research Partners, asked about Journeys' current performance and comp assumptions. Vaughn responded that quarter-to-date comps were tracking mid-single digits but guidance for the full year was below that, reflecting expected peaks and valleys. Vaughn indicated, "We expect a higher comp in the early part of the year... and then, of course, in the back part of the year... we're mindful of that."
  • Kummetz asked about changes to Journeys' assortment and new brands. Vaughn explained that growth is expected from existing franchises, with opportunity across about 10 different brands but overall growth is "not dependent on adding anything new."
  • Kummetz inquired about Schuh's gross margin pressure and expected recovery. Vaughn said, "We are going to withdraw from promotions. We won't get there all the way this year, but we will make a really good dent into it."
  • Joseph Civello, Truist Securities, asked about the canvas category and premium brand engagement. Vaughn noted that canvas remains important but is not expected to grow, while premium product and brand activations are set to drive higher ASPs.
  • Samuel Poser, Williams Trading, LLC, requested specifics on store opening/closing timing and 4.0 store rollout. Vaughn said 80 more 4.0 stores are planned, reaching about 20% of the fleet, with expansions into larger footprints and Kids 4.0 tests. Harris added that openings will be weighted to Johnston & Murphy in the back half, with closures primarily in Journeys in Q1 and Q2.
  • Mantero Moreno-Cheek, Jefferies, asked about traffic, ticket, inventory, and 4.0 store comps. Vaughn confirmed traffic is down but conversion and ASP are up, and 4.0 stores are comping 25%+ above the rest of the chain.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on sustainability of Journeys' comps, margin recovery at Schuh, product assortment evolution, and timing of store initiatives. Their tone was probing but constructive, seeking detailed breakdowns and actionable guidance.
  • Management conveyed confidence in strategic initiatives and turnaround momentum, repeatedly emphasizing ongoing cost discipline, market share gains, and the strength of the multichannel model. Vaughn's tone during prepared remarks was upbeat and assertive, while in Q&A she was detailed but cautious about near-term volatility: "We won't get there all the way this year, but we will make a really good dent into it."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained consistent, but management's tone was more optimistic, with greater emphasis on execution, cost leverage, and the rollout of new strategic pillars.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from caution last quarter, when the outlook was revised downward due to UK and license headwinds, to a more constructive tone focused on profitability improvement and strategic resets for Schuh and Brand Group.
  • Journeys momentum remains a core theme, but this quarter management outlined additional expansion for 4.0 stores and further digital investments, compared to last quarter's focus on stabilizing e-commerce comps and brand campaign launches.
  • Schuh remains under pressure, but the Q4 call details a deliberate move to curtail promotions and focus on gross margin recovery, whereas last quarter the discussion centered on matching competitor discounts.
  • Management's confidence in the multichannel model and cost discipline has strengthened, while analysts’ focus continues to be on key drivers of comp and gross margin recovery.

Risks and Concerns

  • Schuh's profitability is challenged by a highly promotional UK market; management is prioritizing margin over short-term comps and expects only partial recovery this year.
  • Ongoing tariff exposure is expected to have a net negative operating income impact of $5 million to $10 million, with mitigation efforts in place.
  • License exits and related revenue impacts will continue to weigh on Brand Group through the transition to Wrangler, with the most pressure in Q2 and Q3.
  • Store closures will reduce sales but are expected to be accretive to operating income.
  • Quarterly earnings comparisons will be distorted by tax rate volatility, particularly in Q1 and Q2, due to the tax valuation allowance.

Final Takeaway

Genesco ended fiscal 2026 with robust momentum, highlighted by strong comp growth at Journeys, cost discipline, and the accelerating rollout of its 4.0 store concept. The company is targeting adjusted EPS of $1.90 to $2.30 for fiscal 2027, underpinned by further gains at Journeys, a deliberate reset to restore Schuh profitability, and continued focus on operational efficiencies. While headwinds remain in the UK market and from ongoing license transitions, management emphasized its conviction in the "Footwear First" strategy and outlined multiple growth drivers to support sustained margin and earnings improvement in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Accenture ticks up even as Deutsche Bank
data.description:
Accenture (ACN) shares ticked up 0.5% in Friday trading even as Deutsche Bank cut its price target on the technology consulting firm, citing a cautious view on the space.

“On March 19, before the open, we expect ACN to report 2Q26 revenue of ~$17.8bn (~3% cc, ~2% organic cc) and adj. EPS of $2.84,” analysts at the firm wrote in a note to clients. “Our cautious view on the IT Services sector remains unchanged heading into the print, as the industry faces pressures from a soft cyclical backdrop. This view has been echoed by industry participants, suggesting the CY26 selling environment is not likely to materially improve vs. last year’s levels. Further, recent geopolitical headlines likely create incremental caution around corporate IT spend.”

The analysts lowered their price target on Accenture to $230 from $280 but kept their Hold rating on the stock.

Additionally, the analysts said they will be paying attention to hear if Accenture has any updates to artificial intelligence adoption trends and if there has been movement from proof of concept to scaled solutions. Other focal points will be on the company's federal business, notable given the uncertain macro and geopolitical environment.

“While we expect a moderate beat from ACN in 2Q26 and slight upside to where our current 2H26 estimates sit, given how the stock has traded and investor demand for continued beats and raises through the rest of FY26, we expect ACN will opt to raise the low end of its current FY26 guide to ~3-5% cc (from ~2-5% cc currently) while flowing through any adj. EPS beat in the [quarter],” the analysts added.

data.title: DTI outlines $155M–$170M 2026 revenue target as Eastern Hemisphere growth accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Drilling Tools International Corporation (DTI) (DTI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • R. Prejean, CEO, reported strong performance in the fourth quarter, emphasizing "our ability to deliver consistent returns in the face of continued market softness." He highlighted that "DTI's annual adjusted free cash flow has grown each year since going public in 2023," and noted 2025's results were "at or above the high end of our guidance ranges." He stated, "We generated total rental revenues of $129.6 million and total product sales revenues of $30.1 million, or $159.6 million on a consolidated basis. Adjusted net income for 2025 was $3.4 million and adjusted diluted EPS for 2025 was $0.10 per share." Prejean pointed to the company's fourth acquisition since going public and a reduction of net debt by over $11 million in the back half of 2025. He credited Eastern Hemisphere expansion, with revenue in that region up 78% year-over-year and now representing 14% of total revenue, as a key driver of resilience.
  • David Johnson, CFO, stated, "we are pleased to have achieved another record year for adjusted free cash flow." He reported $3.6 million in cash and cash equivalents, net debt of $42.2 million, and a net leverage ratio of 1.1x at year-end. Johnson added, "Fourth quarter tool rental revenue was $30.4 million, and product sales revenue totaled $8.1 million. Net income attributable to stockholders for the fourth quarter was $1.2 million or $0.03 per share." He highlighted share buyback activities and cost control, saying, "We purchased approximately $660,000 of additional common shares, averaging $2.17 per share."
  • Prejean addressed the Middle East conflict, stating, "Our Middle East personnel were all accounted for, have sheltered in place per local government requirements and are maintaining continuity with customers' needs and supporting our operations. We have experienced minimal disruption to our ongoing business thus far."

Outlook

  • Johnson announced 2026 full year guidance: "2026 revenue is expected to be in the range of $155 million to $170 million. Adjusted EBITDA is expected to be within the range of $35 million to $45 million. Capital expenditures are expected to be between $18 million and $23 million. And finally, we expect our 2026 adjusted free cash flow to range between $17 million to $22 million." He clarified, "We have constructed these ranges with the assumption that activity will remain relatively flat in the first half of 2026 and improved slightly in the second half of the year."
  • Prejean reiterated growth catalysts not built into guidance, including "rig reactivations in Saudi Arabia, incremental tenders in the broader Middle East and increased project activity in select international markets."

Financial Results

  • The company reported consolidated Q4 revenue of $38.5 million. Q4 adjusted net income was $1.5 million, with adjusted diluted EPS of $0.04. Fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA was $10.1 million, and adjusted free cash flow was $6.1 million. Capital expenditures in Q4 were $4 million, with maintenance CapEx at approximately 10% of total revenue.
  • For the full year 2025, adjusted EBITDA was $39.3 million and adjusted free cash flow reached $19.2 million, with net debt reduction and ongoing share buybacks highlighted as capital deployment priorities.

Q&A

  • Steve Ferazani, Sidoti: Asked about the strong Q4 EBITDA margin and drivers behind it. David Johnson responded, "it was just a combination of we didn't see all the Q4 sort of typical seasonal softness in some of our numbers. And then we were further benefiting from some of the cost reductions that we did earlier in the year...our product kind of mix was a little bit different."
  • Ferazani asked about product mix impacts. Johnson replied, "mainly it was a product sale impact. We had some additional product sale that's a little bit better kind of margin profile, especially on the [Lawson hole DBR] type sales."
  • Ferazani inquired about strong free cash flow. Johnson noted, "a lot of our CapEx is front-loaded in the year...our third quarter was stronger than the first and second quarter and then our fourth quarter was even stronger on the free cash flow side for that reason."
  • Ferazani questioned M&A and debt reduction priorities. Prejean stated, "we have a healthy pipeline of M&A opportunities...Our use of funds, as they flow, will be debt service, M&A, some buybacks, but mostly the year -- throughout '25, we focused on integration and gaining efficiencies."
  • Ferazani asked about growth in the Eastern Hemisphere and APAC. Prejean explained, "we're getting lots of opportunities throughout Africa...despite the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, we're able to continue maintaining our customer support. Surprisingly, most everyone is still in operation...our Malaysian entity up and running with our Asia Pac focus...all of those things are starting to get traction in Europe, Middle East and Asia Pac."
  • Ferazani asked about US revenue per active rig. Prejean said, "we model it as a steady state with opportunistic realities where there's -- some of our new technologies gain traction...mostly the market is a steady-state environment."
  • Ferazani and John Daniel (Daniel Energy Partners) both raised concerns about the Middle East conflict's impact. Prejean confirmed, "it's unknown today. All we know today is that things are still operating...so far, our team has performed just fantastic."
  • Daniel asked about 2026 guidance timing. Prejean confirmed, "that was all created pre-Iran."
  • Daniel asked about debt reduction allocation for 2026. Prejean responded, "one could expect continued pay down majority of the debt. Hopefully, we could probably accelerate that, but it will depend on the occurrences that happen throughout the year."
  • Daniel probed for management stance amid market turmoil. Prejean said, "it's a very dynamic and fluid situation...we're flexible in -- with regard to the opportunity that may present itself as a result of this conflict."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on cash flow, margins, and geographic expansion, while pressing for clarity on conflict risks and M&A. Their questions reflected cautious optimism but included repeated risk checks.
  • Management’s tone was confident in prepared remarks, emphasizing achievements and growth, but became more cautious in response to geopolitical queries, often referencing flexibility and scenario planning. Prejean used phrases like "it's unknown today" and "we're flexible" when addressing uncertainties, indicating a measured approach.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained watchful, and management showed increased caution regarding geopolitical risks while maintaining an optimistic outlook on operational resilience and growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 moved upward at the midpoint compared to 2025, with a new revenue range of $155 million to $170 million and adjusted EBITDA of $35 million to $45 million, reflecting a slight increase in confidence despite persistent market uncertainty.
  • The Eastern Hemisphere contribution grew from 15% in Q3 to 14% in Q4 (noting full-year context), with year-over-year growth in that segment accelerating.
  • Management tone shifted from resilience in a challenging cycle to cautious optimism, with additional focus on crisis management due to the Middle East conflict.
  • Analysts' focus remained on operational metrics, cash flow, and risk, but with increased scrutiny on geopolitical exposures and capital allocation strategies.
  • Management's confidence in operational integration and capital discipline was consistent, but language around risk and flexibility became more prominent.

Risks and Concerns

  • The ongoing conflict in the Middle East was cited as a risk, with Prejean detailing, "We have experienced minimal disruption to our ongoing business thus far...We are diligently monitoring the situation and have launched our crisis response plan."
  • Management acknowledged that 2026 guidance was constructed before recent escalations and emphasized flexibility in capital deployment and operational response depending on how the situation evolves.
  • Analysts highlighted concerns around revenue exposure to the Middle East and the potential for operational disruptions, as well as the impact of oil market volatility on service pricing.

Final Takeaway

DTI closed 2025 with robust results, highlighted by resilient free cash flow, significant debt reduction, and strong Eastern Hemisphere growth. The company outlined 2026 guidance reflecting steady revenue expansion and continued capital discipline. Management acknowledged current geopolitical risks, particularly in the Middle East, but indicated that minimal disruption has occurred to date, and crisis plans are in place. The outlook signals a commitment to operational excellence, ongoing M&A evaluation, and readiness to capitalize on emerging opportunities, all supported by a flexible and diversified global platform.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil crosses $90/bbl and energy ETFs such as XLE step into focus
data.description:
Energy focused exchange traded funds moved into the spotlight Friday afternoon as crude oil (CL1:COM) prices climbed above $90 per barrel, marking the first time in more than two years the commodity has traded at that level.

Oil has not reached the $90 threshold since October 2023, and the latest move higher has drawn renewed investor attention to energy equities and sector-linked funds. 

Despite the strength in oil, broader markets remained under pressure. All 11 sectors of the S&P 500 (SP500) were trading lower on the session, with the benchmark index down roughly 0.9%. Energy stocks, however, showed relative resilience. The sector slipped only about 0.1% on the day, making it the strongest-performing group within the index as investors rotated toward companies benefiting from higher crude prices. 

Among the most closely watched funds is the Energy Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLE), managed by State Street. With nearly $40B in assets under management, XLE stands as the largest energy focused exchange traded fund available to investors and is often used as a benchmark for the performance of major U.S. energy companies. 

The fund holds a concentrated portfolio of 26 large-cap oil and gas producers, refiners, and energy infrastructure firms. Below is a look at XLE’s top 10 holdings, ranked by portfolio weighting, along with each company’s year-to-date performance. 

  • Exxon Mobil (XOM), +26% YTD.
  • Chevron (CVX), +24.3% YTD.
  • ConocoPhillips (COP), +26.1% YTD.
  • Williams Companies (WMB), +23.8% YTD.
  • SLB (SLB), +23.1% YTD.
  • EOG Resources (EOG), +26.1% YTD.
  • Kinder Morgan (KMI), +21.5% YTD.
  • Baker Hughes (BKR), +32.8% YTD.
  • Valero Energy (VLO), +36.7% YTD.
  • Phillips 66 (PSX), +25.9% YTD.

Furthermore, see other energy and oil and gas related funds that are in focus as oil tops $90/bbl. 

Energy ETFs: (VDE), (XOP), (AMLP), (OIH), and (IXC). 

Oil ETFs: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL). 

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL).

data.title: Weekly performance: Bitcoin pulls back after $74,000 rally
data.description:
• At the time of writing, the Bitcoin price was trading near $68,566, showing a 4% drop in the last 24 hours. However, if we look at the bigger picture, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) still had a weekly gain of around 7%, which means the overall weekly trend remained positive.
  • At the beginning of the week, Bitcoin started trading between $68,000 and $69,000. Soon after, buying activity increased and pushed the price above the $71,000 level. 
  • The upward momentum continued through the middle of the week, helping $BTC reach a weekly high close to $73,800–$74,000. This was one of the strongest price moves seen during the week.
  • However, the rally didn’t hold. As the price of Bitcoin was nearing the mark of $74,000, profit-taking kicked in, which increased selling pressure and pulled the price back to around $69,500–70,000 by March 6.
  • From a technical point of view, $73,500–$74,000 worked as a strong resistance level. On the other hand, $67,000 is currently acting as the main support level.
  • Institutional activity also played a role in the market during the week. According to data from SoSoValue, Bitcoin spot ETFs saw a net outflow of about $228 million on March 5, which added short-term selling pressure. However, the total weekly ETF inflows reached around $917 million, showing that institutional interest is still strong.
  • Other factors also affected Bitcoin’s rise & fall pattern. Several mining companies increased the selling pressure. Firms like Cango (CANG) sold 4,451 BTC in February, Core Scientific (CORZ) plans to sell approximately 2,500 BTC in Q1, and Bitdeer Technologies Group (BTDR) and Riot Platforms (RIOT) have also sold or plan to sell some of their Bitcoin holdings.
  • In addition, talks on landmark crypto legislation have hit a new impasse after banks said they could not back a compromise pushed by the White House, a development that cast doubt on whether the bill will pass this year and sparked criticism from President Donald Trump, who accused lenders of trying to undermine it.
  • The ongoing US–Iran conflict also adds uncertainty to Bitcoin's price.
  • Overall, Bitcoin had a strong rally in the middle of the week but later saw a correction. For now, holding above the $67,000 support level will be important. If the price goes below this level, it could fall towards the range of $65,000–$66,000 in the short term.

data.title: U.S. stocks' decline persists as nonfarm payrolls contract, oil prices accelerate
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages continued to decline on Friday after nonfarm payrolls unexpectedly contracted in February, and oil prices continue to rise.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.2%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.9%, and the Dow (DJI) was -1.3%.

U.S. nonfarm payrolls dropped by 92,000 in February, vs. the 60,000 consensus—a sharp reversal from the 126,000 jobs added in January, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

“January bounced on the stronger side, February bounced on the weaker side. This is a shift towards labor market worries but only a very small shift. Mostly it is what a world with usual volatility around low supply growth will look like. Fed should still wait and see,” said Jason Furman, economist and former U.S. chairman of the Council of Economic Advisers.

In addition, U.S. retail sales declined by 0.2% MoM to $733.5B in January, vs. the 0.4% fall expected, the U.S. Census Bureau reported.

Oil prices (CL1:COM) climbed to $90/bbl on Friday as investors weighed escalating geopolitical tensions in the Middle East following military confrontations between the U.S. and Iran. Brent futures (CO1:COM) rose to $92.

Moreover, U.S. President Donald Trump said the U.S. should play a role in selecting Iran’s next leader after the death of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in recent U.S.-Israeli strikes. He also said there will be no deal to end the U.S.-Israel war against Iran without an "unconditional surrender."

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) dropped 1 basis point to 4.13%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) lost 4 basis points to 3.55%.

data.title: White House says U.S. has enough munitions for Iran war, but pushing for more
data.description:
The White House said that while the U.S. has sufficient weapons and munitions to achieve its military objectives in Iran, President Trump still plans to push defense contractors to speed up production when he meets with them on Friday. 

“The U.S. military has more than enough munitions, ammo, and weapons stockpiles to continue demolishing the Iranian regime and achieve the goals of Operation Epic Fury,” White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said in a statement, according to Bloomberg. 

“Nevertheless, President Trump has always been intensely focused on strengthening our military, which is why this meeting with defense contractors was scheduled weeks ago," Leavitt added.

Trump is set to meet with executives of several of the nation's top defense contractors on Friday, including representatives from RTX (RTX), Lockheed Martin (LMT), Boeing (BA), Northrop Grumman (NOC), Honeywell (HON), and L3Harris Technologies (LHX), Bloomberg said.

“The President will continue to call on these U.S. companies to more speedily build American-made weapons, which are the absolute best in the world,” Leavitt added.

data.title: Cohen & Company signals robust 2026 pipeline with focus on expanding investment banking and targets $60M–$65M in fixed income trading revenue
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cohen & Company Inc. (COHN) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Lester Brafman stated that the company delivered a strong fourth quarter and full year 2025, driven by "the continued expansion of our client franchise and particularly our full-service boutique investment bank, Cohen & Company Capital Markets, which continues to focus on frontier technologies, including digital assets, energy transition and natural resources."
  • Brafman underscored leadership team strengthening in energy transition, space technology, aerospace, and communications infrastructure, and highlighted $43 billion in closed transactions for 2025, including a #1 ranking in SPAC IPO underwritings and de-SPAC advisory per SPAC Research.
  • Brafman announced, "For the full year of 2025, basic and fully diluted net income attributable to Cohen & Company per share was $8.33 and $4.35, respectively. Total revenue was $275.6 million, an increase of 246% from 2024 and adjusted pretax income of $41.4 million, representing 15% of total revenue."
  • Brafman revealed a special dividend of $0.70 per share and a regular quarterly dividend of $0.25 per share, in addition to a previously announced $2 per share special dividend paid in January 2026.
  • CFO Joseph Pooler said, "Our net income attributable to Cohen & Company Inc. shareholders was $8.1 million for the quarter or $1.48 per fully diluted share compared to net income of $4.6 million for the prior quarter or $2.58 per fully diluted share and net loss of $2 million for the prior year quarter or $1.21 per fully diluted share."
  • Pooler added, "Our adjusted pretax income was $18.3 million for the quarter compared to adjusted pretax income of $16.4 million for the prior quarter and adjusted pretax loss of $7.7 million for the prior year quarter."

Outlook

  • Brafman indicated that "first quarter 2026 revenue [is] trending substantially higher than first quarter 2025" and described the transaction pipeline as "more robust than it was a year ago."
  • Management emphasized continued focus on being the adviser of choice to growth in frontier technology sectors and expanding the investment bank footprint, with specific attention to building out industry verticals in space, aerospace, telecommunications, and energy.

Financial Results

  • Cohen & Company Capital Markets (CCM) generated $50.8 million in revenue in the fourth quarter and $184 million for the full year 2025, a 370% increase over the prior year, accounting for 67% of total company revenue for 2025.
  • Fourth quarter investment banking and new issue revenue was $55 million, with $50.8 million from CCM, primarily from SPAC M&A and IPO transactions. European insurance origination contributed $3.6 million, and commercial real estate origination $300,000.
  • Principal transactions and other revenue reached $31.5 million, mainly due to the completion of the Columbus Circle Capital Corp. I and ProCap Financial business combination, which resulted in $33 million of principal transactions revenue and associated compensation and noncontrolling interest expenses.
  • Net trading revenue for the quarter was $13.8 million, while asset management revenue was $2.7 million. Compensation and benefits expense rose to $57.8 million due to variable incentive compensation and founder share allocations.
  • Total equity at year-end was $103.1 million, with consolidated corporate indebtedness at $33 million. The company ended the year with 126 employees.

Q&A

  • Michael Grondahl, Northland Securities, Inc.: Asked for details on the transaction pipeline and standout sectors. Brafman responded, "if I was on this call a year ago and looking at where our pipeline is, we're ahead of where we were last year...we dominate in the SPAC and the de-SPAC space...from what we've printed in the SPAC space, we've got some traditional M&A mandates, some capital raises, capital market advisory work."
  • Grondahl: Inquired about top 2026 priorities. Brafman said priorities are "expanding our investment bank...getting more verticals and not being as dependent on the SPAC product" and "continue to grow our [fixed income trading] footprint...revenue-wise, it was close to $50 million this year, and we'd like to get that up to $60 million, $65 million or so."
  • Grondahl: Requested MD headcount details. Brafman estimated adding "another 2 to 3 through promotions and hiring over the year, maybe as much -- maybe as many as 4 or 5," with Pooler noting the investment bank had 28 total employees at year-end and anticipated growth of about 5 in 2026.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed positive sentiment, congratulating management on annual results and seeking details on growth areas, pipeline, and headcount expansion.
  • Management's tone was confident and constructive, emphasizing robust pipeline growth, sector leadership, and strategic expansion. Brafman consistently used assertive language about current momentum and future opportunities.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's confidence has increased, with more specific forward-looking statements about revenue momentum and sector focus. Analysts maintained a positive, inquisitive tone in both quarters.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from general confidence to explicit forecasts of higher Q1 2026 revenue and a more robust transaction pipeline.
  • Strategic focus broadened beyond SPACs, with increased emphasis on diversifying into new industry verticals and expanding fixed income trading.
  • Key metrics such as full year total revenue, CCM revenue, and profit margins all increased significantly quarter-over-quarter, as did compensation and workforce size.
  • Management sentiment has become more optimistic and forward-looking, with analysts continuing to focus on pipeline strength and growth strategy.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that future dividend decisions "may be impacted by quarterly operating results and the company's capital needs."
  • Pooler referenced fluctuations in compensation expenses linked to variable incentive compensation and founder share allocations.
  • Loss from equity method affiliates was $5.1 million, driven by mark-to-market losses on SPAC series fund investments.
  • Management reminded investors that the CCM business can be uneven from quarter to quarter and that realized or unrealized gains or losses from investment instruments received as consideration could impact results.

Final Takeaway

Cohen & Company closed 2025 with record revenue, significant expansion in its investment banking franchise, and a growing pipeline across frontier technology sectors. Management highlighted ongoing diversification beyond SPACs, robust compensation and equity growth, and clear priorities for further vertical and fixed income expansion in 2026. The company remains confident about maintaining momentum and driving sustainable value for stockholders, supported by a special dividend and a strong start for the new year.
